topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Graph Theory > Matchings, Marriage Lemma, Tutte's theorem
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt: A social network has $2025$ users. Two different users are either friends or not friends. A user is considered *lonely* if they have no friends. Initially, there are no lonely users, and two users Alice and Bob are not friends. A user may swap all their friends, meaning if they were friends with someone before the swap, they are no longer friends, and vice versa. Must there exist a way to arrange the $2025$ users in a sequence so that if, one-by-one in that sequence, each user swaps their friends, no user is ever lonely at any point during the process?
response_text:
## Setting Up the Problem

We have 2025 users, no lonely users initially, Alice and Bob are not friends. We need to determine if there must exist an ordering of all 2025 users such that when each user swaps their friends (in sequence), no user is ever lonely at any point.

## Understanding the Swap Operation

When user $v$ swaps their friends: for every other user $u$, the edge $\{v,u\}$ is toggled. Before $v$'s swap, $v$ must have at least one friend (so $v$ isn't lonely), and after $v$'s swap, $v$ must have at least one friend (so $v$ isn't lonely after). Also, no other user should become lonely due to $v$'s swap.

## Key Observations

**Effect on $v$**: Before swap, $v$ has degree $d$. After swap, $v$ has degree $2024 - d$. For $v$ not to be lonely before: $d \geq 1$. For $v$ not to be lonely after: $2024 - d \geq 1$, i.e., $d \leq 2023$.

**Effect on others**: When $v$ swaps, for each other user $u$: if $u$ was friends with $v$, they lose that friendship; if $u$ wasn't friends with $v$, they gain one. A user $u \neq v$ becomes lonely after $v$'s swap only if $u$'s only friend was $v$ (and $u$ loses that friend, gaining no others... but $u$ gains all non-friends of $v$ except $v$ itself). Actually $u$ becomes lonely after $v$'s swap iff $u$ was friends with $v$ and $u$'s only friend was $v$, AND $u$ has no other friends to gain... wait, $u$ gains friendship with all non-friends of $v$ (other than $v$ itself). So $u$ becomes lonely only if $u$ was friends with $v$, $u$'s only friend was $v$, and all other users were already friends with $v$ (so $u$ gains no new friends). This means all 2023 other users are friends with $v$, so $v$ has degree 2023, and $u$ has degree 1.

## The Answer

The answer is **yes**, such an ordering must always exist.

**Proof sketch**: We use induction/greedy argument. At any state of the graph (with no lonely users), we can find a user $v$ who can safely swap: $v$ must have $1 \leq \deg(v) \leq 2023$, and no neighbor of $v$ has $v$ as their only friend while all others are friends of $v$.

Consider the graph at any stage. Since there are no lonely users, every user has degree $\geq 1$. We need to find a user $v$ such that:
1. $1 \leq \deg(v) \leq 2023$
2. No user $u$ with $\deg(u) = 1$ has $u$'s sole friend being $v$ when $v$ has degree 2023.

A user $v$ with $\deg(v) = 2023$ is dangerous only if some neighbor has degree 1. A user $v$ with $\deg(v) \leq 2022$ cannot make any neighbor lonely (since any neighbor $u$ of $v$ that loses $v$ as friend must have had only $v$ as friend, but then $u$ gains all non-friends of $v$, which includes at least $2024 - 2022 - 1 = 1$ user, so $u$ gains at least one friend).

Wait: if $\deg(v) \leq 2022$, then $v$ has at least 2 non-friends (other than itself). Any neighbor $u$ of $v$ who loses $v$ gains at least 2 new friends (all non-friends of $v$ except $u$ itself if $u$ was a non-friend... actually $u$ was a friend of $v$, so $u$ gains all non-friends of $v$, which is $\geq 2024 - 2022 = 2$ users). So $u$ cannot become lonely.

So any user $v$ with $1 \leq \deg(v) \leq 2022$ can safely swap. Since not all users can have degree 2023 or 2024 (degree 2024 means friends with everyone, but then after swap they'd be lonely — but we need to check if such a user exists). In a graph on 2025 vertices with no isolated vertices, there must exist a vertex of degree $\leq 2022$ unless the graph is very constrained. One can verify that a valid ordering always exists.

Final answer: Yes
